disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Progressive worsening of exertional palpitations over the preceding two months, culminating in today's near-syncope
trigger_question_examples: Have you noticed your heart racing during exercise before today, and has it been getting worse?
Has this happened before, even in a milder way, over the past weeks or months?
Have you had any episodes like this building up recently?
When did you first start noticing your heart racing during exercise?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Yeah, actually now that you ask — I've noticed my heart racing during hard runs for the last couple of months. It felt like it was happening more the last few weeks. I didn't think much of it until today.
disclosure_items.patient_deflection_template: Nah, today was the first time anything like this happened.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: No prodromal warmth, nausea, tunnel vision, or vasovagal trigger (heat, prolonged standing, pain, emotional stress) preceding the loss of near-consciousness — the palpitations came first
trigger_question_examples: Right before you felt like you were going to faint, did you notice any warning signs like feeling hot, sick, or your vision going tunnel-like?
Did the racing heart come before the dizziness, or the other way around?
Was there anything that led up to it, like standing still for a long time or feeling unwell first?
Can you describe exactly what happened in order — what came first?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: No warning like that — the racing heart came first, then almost straight away the dark/woozy feeling. It wasn't like I felt sick or hot beforehand, it just hit me.
disclosure_items.patient_deflection_template: It's all a bit of a blur, I just remember feeling dizzy.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Family history detail: maternal uncle died suddenly and unexpectedly at age 30, cause never explained, no known prior heart diagnosis in him
trigger_question_examples: Is there any family history of heart problems or sudden deaths at a young age?
Has anyone in your family died suddenly or unexpectedly, especially at a younger age?
Tell me about your family's health history, particularly anything heart-related.
Do you know if your uncle had any heart problems before he passed away?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Yeah — my mum's brother died suddenly when he was about 30. We were told his heart just stopped. Nobody ever really explained why, he seemed healthy before that as far as we know.
disclosure_items.patient_deflection_template: Not that I know of, no.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Patient has continued high-intensity training over recent months despite the escalating exertional palpitations, with no prior cardiac work-up
trigger_question_examples: Have you kept training at the same intensity despite noticing your heart racing?
Has anyone ever checked your heart with a scan or ECG before?
Did you cut back on training when you started noticing the racing, or keep going as normal?
Have you had any heart tests done in the past, like an ECG?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: No, I've never had my heart checked before. I just kept training as normal, I didn't think the racing was a big deal, I figured it was just from pushing myself hard.
disclosure_items.patient_deflection_template: I cut back a bit but nothing major.

patient_initiated_exchanges.patient_text: Do you think I just fainted from running too hard? I've heard that can happen to athletes sometimes.
patient_initiated_exchanges.expected_ai_doctor_response_quality: Acknowledge that exertional presyncope can occur in fit athletes, but explain that palpitations preceding the episode plus the family history need to be properly assessed before assuming a benign cause. Should not reassure the patient that this is 'probably normal for an athlete' without further screening — this is the anchoring/availability trap.

patient_initiated_exchanges.patient_text: I have a big race coming up in a few weeks — is it okay if I just take it easy but keep training a bit so I don't lose fitness?
patient_initiated_exchanges.expected_ai_doctor_response_quality: Firmly but empathetically advise complete cessation of strenuous exercise and competitive sport until cardiology assessment is complete — do not agree to 'reduced intensity' training as a compromise, since exertion is the trigger for the concerning symptoms and the family history raises sudden cardiac death risk.

deflection_behaviours.description: When asked about his family history, patient briefly expresses distress about how upsetting his uncle's death was for the family before answering — tests whether the AI Doctor stays supportive while still extracting the clinically relevant detail (age, suddenness, cause unknown).
deflection_behaviours.deflection_text_template: It was really hard on my mum when it happened, she still doesn't like talking about it much... but yeah, he was about 30, and it was totally out of nowhere.
